text: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy of a hemorrhagic pheochromocytoma. Authors have nothing to disclose. There are sixty-five reported cases of hemorrhagic pheochromocytoma. Emergent surgical mortality has been reported to be as high as twenty-nine to thirty-four percent. Survival has significantly increased with pre-medication and elective surgery.

Our patient is a forty-six year old female with no prior medical history who presented to an outside hospital with a day of nausea and acute right-sided back pain. She reported three years of intermittent headache, palpitation, and a hundred pound weight loss over the past year. She endorsed a blood pressure of two hundred six over one forty-five and a heart rate of one forty-one as well as a diffuse abdominal tenderness. She was in diabetic ketoacidosis with a rapid decrease of her hemoglobin and suffered from acute liver and kidney failure. She was intubated, treated with nicartapine, and packed red blood cell. She was then transferred to the Mount Sinai Hospital.

At Mount Sinai, a CT angiogram was emergently performed. It showed a large, nine-centimeter hemorrhagic adrenal mass in the right retroperitoneum, as shown in the image. Her adrenal function laboratory analysis was consistent with pheochromocytoma. After stabilization, she was discharged on phenoxybenzamine, insulin, and propranolol. Elective adrenalectomy was performed two months after her discharge. She was placed in a left lateral decubitus position. Four ports were placed along the left costal margin, as shown here.

A laparoscopic liver retractor is used to retract the right lobe of the liver, exposing the large adrenal mass. The surgeon incises the peritoneal reflection up to the diaphragm, including the triangular ligament. Dissection is performed between the adrenal gland and the inferior vena cava to expose the right adrenal vein, which is then carefully isolated.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to carefully divide the isolated right adrenal vein. The medial dissection is extended upwards, with attention to small vessels arising from the inferior vena cava. A second adrenal vein is encountered, isolated, and divided.

The surgeon mobilizes the inferior and lateral attachments of the adrenal gland, ensuring complete isolation with a rim of periadrenal fatty tissue. The posterior adrenal gland is dissected with care due to previous hemorrhage, using a suction device to control bleeding. Alternating dissections between lateral and superior attachments allow the gland to be lifted, achieving hemostasis. The tumor is placed in a retrieval sac and extracted, with negative margins confirmed.
